restore-without-rewriting-history.label: Restore without Rewriting History
restore-without-rewriting-history.summary: Correct working-tree and staging mistakes at the layer where they occurred, without changing existing commits.

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.title: Discard One Unstaged Mistake
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

`notes.txt` has an unwanted working-tree edit. Restore it from HEAD, then verify that the repository is clean.
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.hint: Return the working file to the recorded version only after confirming the local edit is unwanted.

help.concept: git restore PATH replaces an unstaged working-tree version with the version recorded in the index/HEAD.
help.hint_1: This operation targets the working tree, not existing commits.
help.hint_2: Verify that the unstaged patch disappears while unrelated staged work remains.

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

`notes.txt` has an unwanted working-tree edit. Restore it from HEAD, then verify that the repository is clean.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf release-kit
mkdir -p release-kit
git -C release-kit init -q -b main
git -C release-kit config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C release-kit config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd release-kit
printf %s 'Approved release note.
' > notes.txt
cd ..
git -C release-kit add -A
git -C release-kit commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd release-kit
printf %s 'Accidental replacement.
' > notes.txt
cd ..

cd release-kit
printf %s 'Approved release note.
' > notes.txt
cd ..
git -C release-kit add -A
git -C release-kit commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd release-kit
printf %s 'Accidental replacement.
' > notes.txt
cd ..

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.title: Unstage Without Losing the Edit
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

`draft.txt` is staged, but it should not be in the next commit. Unstage it while keeping its working-tree content, then read short status.
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.hint: Change the staging decision without discarding the file’s contents.

help.concept: git restore --staged moves the selected path out of the index while preserving the working-tree edit.
help.hint_1: The path should move out of the proposed snapshot and remain in the working tree.
help.hint_2: Read both status columns afterward to verify the layer changed.

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

`draft.txt` is staged, but it should not be in the next commit. Unstage it while keeping its working-tree content, then read short status.

zoeskoul_setup_sh.content:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -eu
rm -rf release-kit
mkdir -p release-kit
git -C release-kit init -q -b main
git -C release-kit config --local user.name "ZoeSkoul Learner"
git -C release-kit config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"
cd release-kit
printf %s 'Draft v1
' > draft.txt
cd ..
git -C release-kit add -A
git -C release-kit commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd release-kit
printf %s 'Draft v2
' > draft.txt
cd ..
git -C release-kit add -- draft.txt

git -C release-kit config --local user.email "learner@zoeskoul.local"

printf %s 'Draft v1
' > draft.txt
cd ..
git -C release-kit add -A
git -C release-kit commit -q -m 'Add project baseline'
cd release-kit
printf %s 'Draft v2
' > draft.txt
cd ..
git -C release-kit add -- draft.txt

1.message: Did you use the compact status view and read the entry for `draft.txt`?

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.title: Unstage, Then Discard
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.prompt:
You are working in the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

`summary.txt` is staged with an accidental rewrite. First unstage it, then restore the working file from HEAD and confirm a clean state.
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.hint: Change the staging decision without discarding the file’s contents.

help.concept: Staged and working-tree state are separate boundaries, so a staged mistake may require two deliberate restore commands.
help.hint_1: The path should move out of the proposed snapshot and remain in the working tree.
help.hint_2: Read both status columns afterward to verify the layer changed.

try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.solutionCode:
git restore --staged summary.txt
git restore summary.txt
git status --short
try_restore_without_rewriting_history_sk.instructions:
You are working in the prepared `release-kit/` repository.

`summary.txt` is staged with an accidental rewrite. First unstage it, then restore the working file from HEAD and confirm a clean state.

single-choice-1.title: Purpose of git restore
single-choice-1.prompt: What is the primary purpose of the `git restore` command?
single-choice-1.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Correct working-tree and staging mistakes at the right layer while leaving existing commits untouched.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: To revert changes in the working directory
options.b: To commit changes to the repository

single-choice-2.title: Effect of git restore --staged
single-choice-2.prompt: What happens when you use `git restore --staged` on a file?
single-choice-2.hint: Focus on what the command or concept observes or changes at this point in the workflow.

help.concept: Correct working-tree and staging mistakes at the right layer while leaving existing commits untouched.
help.hint_1: Remove options that act on a different repository layer than the one named in the question.
help.hint_2: Choose the answer that remains accurate even when other unrelated files exist.

options.a: The file is removed from the staging area but changes are kept
options.b: The file is deleted from the repository
options.c: The file is committed to the repository
options.d: The file is reverted to the last committed state

multi-choice-1.title: Choosing the Correct Restore Command
multi-choice-1.prompt: Which commands can be used to revert changes in the working directory and unstage a file?
multi-choice-1.hint: Judge each option independently; choose only the statements that stay true in this exact repository state.

help.concept: Correct working-tree and staging mistakes at the right layer while leaving existing commits untouched.
help.hint_1: A partly true option is still wrong if it adds an effect Git does not guarantee.
help.hint_2: Use the layer named in the prompt—working tree, staging area, or history—to test each choice.

drag-reorder-1.title: Discard a Change That Is Already Staged
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the safe correction steps for a file whose accidental rewrite is both staged and present in the working tree.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Start with the state that must exist before the next action can make sense.

help.concept: Correct working-tree and staging mistakes at the right layer while leaving existing commits untouched.
help.hint_1: Track how the project moves from working files to a proposed snapshot and then to history.
help.hint_2: Read the ordered steps as one continuous repository story.

tokens.t1: Remove the accidental version from the staging area
tokens.t2: Restore the working file from the recorded version

restore-sketch-0.title: Restore an Unstaged Working Copy
restore-sketch-0.bodyMarkdown:
When an unwanted edit exists only in the working tree, restore the file from the staged or committed version:

```bash
git restore notes.txt
```

Review first, because the discarded local text will not enter history. The existing commits remain unchanged.

restore-sketch-1.title: Unstage Without Losing the Edit
restore-sketch-1.bodyMarkdown:
A correct edit may be staged for the wrong commit. Move it out of the proposal while preserving the working file:

```bash
git restore --staged draft.txt
```

Short status should move the mark from the staging column to the working-tree column.

restore-sketch-2.title: Correct Both Layers in Order
restore-sketch-2.bodyMarkdown:
When an accidental rewrite is already staged, correct the staging area first and the working tree second:

```bash
git restore --staged summary.txt
git restore summary.txt
```

The order preserves a clear source for the final restore. It changes current state only; history stays intact.
